petitioner_background: The petitioner is a beach volleyball player from Canada seeking classification as an individual of extraordinary ability in athletics under EB-1A (INA § 203(b)(1)(A)). She competed on the Canadian national team/high performance beach volleyball program, represented Canada in FIVB World Tour events (38 tournaments since 2011, 50 World Tour events with 34 top-9 finishes), and served as an alternative athlete for Canada at the 2016 Olympics. She claimed a second-place ranking in an international federation database and received Top Rookie (2014) and Most Improved Player (2017) awards from an international volleyball federation.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Petitioner submitted evidence that in 2017 she won a gold medal and a bronze medal at two FIVB events. A list of statistics from the Beach Volleyball Database showed that from 2009 through 2017 she won three silver medals and two additional bronze medals at FIVB events. She also submitted articles from CBC Sports and the Toronto Star covering her receipt of the 2017 gold and bronze medals, including one article stating the gold was 'Canada's fourth overall on the international circuit since the formation of the women's [tour] in 1992.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that together with the media coverage in the record, the petitioner established that these awards are nationally recognized. The combination of the medals won at international federation events and the coverage in major Canadian media (CBC Sports, Toronto Star) was sufficient to establish national recognition of the prizes for excellence in the field. The AAO concluded she met this criterion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Petitioner claimed membership based on being a competitor on the Canadian national team and in the FIVB. She submitted a letter from the beach high performance director stating she 'has represented [Canada] in the [FIVB] in 38 different tournaments since 2011' and that Canada 'nominates athletes to compete on the [tour] based on their ability as a beach volleyball player.' She also referenced the FIVB regulations, asserting only members of a state federation's national team are eligible to play in FIVB events, and argued the FIVB 'is comprised of the top professional players in the field that have World Championship ranking.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the record did not contain information about the selection criteria Canada uses to nominate players to compete, nor about the qualifications of those judging the athletes. Regarding FIVB membership, the AAO quoted the FIVB regulations which state that any player who fulfils requirements of rules on nationality, age, gender and other criteria is eligible for participation, conditioned on complying with affiliation principles — the regulation does not require outstanding achievements. The record also did not contain the rules of the Canadian national team to establish its selection required outstanding achievements judged by recognized national/international experts. The AAO concluded she did not meet this criterion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Petitioner submitted articles about her relating to her work in the field published by CBC Sports, the National Post, and the Toronto Star, with evidence demonstrating these publications constitute major media.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that the articles were about the petitioner, related to her work in the field, and that sufficient evidence demonstrated the publishing outlets (CBC Sports, National Post, Toronto Star) constitute major media. The AAO concluded she met this criterion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Petitioner relied on her second-place FIVB ranking, her Top Rookie and Most Improved Player awards, her characterization as 'one of the few star beach volleyball players ever produced by the High Performance (professional) program of the Canadian national federation,' media articles, and two letters from coaches familiar with her playing discussing her awards and abilities as a player.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that the coach letters discussed her awards and abilities as a player but did not identify any original contribution she made or discuss what impact her accomplishments had on the field. The evidence of awards and media coverage reflected honors she received but did not equate to original contributions because they did not reflect what aspects of her work amounted to original contributions or show the influence/impact she had on others in the field. The AAO rejected her argument that requiring original contributions would eliminate all top professional athletes, noting this is only one of ten criteria and athletes can qualify through other criteria. The petitioner failed to show her contributions rose to major significance in the field as a whole rather than just to a team or project. The AAO concluded she did not meet this criterion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Petitioner claimed she performed in a leading or critical role for Canada (Volleyball Canada) as a competitor on the national team and as an alternative athlete to represent Canada at the 2016 Olympics. On appeal she stated she 'has risen to the top of the ranks of professional volleyball players,' 'has played in 50 World Tour events and has placed in the top 9 spots 34 times,' and that she and her partner 'are Canada's best beach volleyball players currently in the game' preparing for the 2020 Olympics. She submitted a letter from her coach stating 'her ability to adjust and refine her already impressive skill sets, is a big reason why our team was able to reach the semi-finals in seven out of the eight events we competed in; winning one event and earning a medal in 4 of the total events played.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that roles as a player for the Canadian high performance beach volleyball program and Canadian professional beach team represent transitory roles that do not correlate with a leading or critical role to an organization. On appeal, the petitioner did not clearly identify what role she performed for any organization, and the evidence she cited related to competitive successes rather than establishing her role was leading or critical. The coach's letter did not explain her role or impact on the team beyond generalized praise. The petitioner also failed to demonstrate that the Canadian high performance beach volleyball program and Canadian professional beach team possess a distinguished reputation. She did not show her role constituted that of a leader or was of significant importance to the outcome of the organization's activities. The AAO concluded she did not meet this criterion.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed the appeal because the petitioner failed to submit the required initial evidence of either a qualifying one-time achievement (major internationally recognized award) or documents meeting at least three of the ten regulatory criteria. Although the AAO withdrew the Director's finding on the plan-to-work issue and found she met two criteria (awards and published material), she fell short of the three-criteria minimum. The AAO also rejected her claim to a one-time achievement, finding the FIVB Top Rookie and Most Improved Player awards and her ranking were not shown to receive international recognition comparable to awards like the Nobel Prize, Olympic medal, Pulitzer Prize, or Academy Award — the articles were only from Canadian sources and did not establish global media coverage or general public recognition. Because she did not meet the initial evidence threshold, the AAO did not need to address the totality of materials in a final merits determination under Kazarian.
legal_citations: Rijal v. USCIS, 772 F. Supp. 2d 1339 (W.D. Wash. 2011)
Silverman v. Eastrich Multiple Investor Fund, L.P., 51 F.3d 28 (3d Cir. 1995)

USCIS Policy Memorandum PM-602-0005.1, Evaluation of Evidence Submitted with Certain Form I-140 Petitions; Revisions to the Adjudicator's Field Manual (AFM) Chapter 22.1, AFM Update AD11-14 (Dec. 22, 2010)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: The Director failed to address the petitioner's one-time achievement claims, which the petitioner argued on appeal necessitated a remand. The AAO declined to remand, invoking its de novo review authority under Matter of Dhanasar to address new issues not raised or resolved in the initial decision. The decision also contains inconsistent pronoun usage, referring to the petitioner as 'he' in places despite identifying her as female elsewhere.
